coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of layered lipophilic graphene quantum dot over Fe@MgO catalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Rezaii, Ebrahim
Mahkam, Mehrdad
coredata.dc:description: The problem of today's societies is finding a solution for optimal energy storage. Nowadays tribology studies focus on transport and friction lost tools, therefore discussion about lubrication is more than ever before. Additionally, the development of new materials such as graphene and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can pave the way to solve tribological issues. GQDs have been known as one of the most important and attractive lubricating nanomaterials and have dominant application in the tribological field. In this research, by applying changes to the GQD structure, we succeeded in synthesizing layered GQD which Octyl amine functionalized layered GQD over Fe@MgO catalyst. The covalent functionalization was demonstrated with 1H NMR, 13C NMR, TEM, FT-IR, and XRD. X-ray diffraction results showed that the obtained product has a layered structure. Superb tribological properties of yield sample were admitted by four-ball and friction tests.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Lipophilic material
Layered material
Fe@MgO catalyst
Friction
Lubricant
Oil additive

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of layered lipophilic graphene quantum dot (LGQD)
Transmission electron microscopy and DLS analysis of LGQD

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the office of the research vice chancellor of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Lubricant size effect on anti-friction performance.
Fluorescence spectrum of LGQD in 300 nm.
XRD spectrum of (a) LGQD and (b) expanded XRD of LGQD.
(a) TEM image, (b) DLS analysis of LGQD.
The result of 4 ball EP test.
(a) mono layer and (b) multilayer structure of LGQD.
Behavior of LGQD against (a) vertical and (b) horizontal pressure.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of layered lipophilic graphene quantum dot over Fe@MgO catalyst

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chemistry Department, Faculty of Science, Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The problem of today's societies is finding a solution for optimal energy storage. Nowadays tribology studies focus on transport and friction lost tools, therefore discussion about lubrication is more than ever before. Additionally, the development of new materials such as graphene and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) can pave the way to solve tribological issues. GQDs have been known as one of the most important and attractive lubricating nanomaterials and have dominant application in the tribological field. In this research, by applying changes to the GQD structure, we succeeded in synthesizing layered GQD which Octyl amine functionalized layered GQD over Fe@MgO catalyst. The covalent functionalization was demonstrated with 1H NMR, 13C NMR, TEM, FT-IR, and XRD. X-ray diffraction results showed that the obtained product has a layered structure. Superb tribological properties of yield sample were admitted by four-ball and friction tests.

ce:section.ce:para: Friction and wear are two reasons that cause energy losses in mechanical activities. Lubrication is the main subject for improving energy efficiency and mechanical durability. Regardless of the completion of each metal surface, it contains ridges, valleys, asperities and depressions [1,2]. Adding nanomaterials as additives in lubricating base oil, is a rapidly developing research field, because of the different nature of the nanomaterials from traditional bulk materials due to their very small size and their specific surface area [3]. Graphene and Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been known as one of the most important and attractive lubricating nanomaterials and have dominant application in tribological field [4–6]. Graphene is an effective nano-filler for a lubricant or grease composition [7]. Graphene must be able to form a stable, uniform dispersion in a lubricating fluid or oil base. In other words, proper dispersion of graphene in a fluid would be a prerequisite to achieve good electrical, thermal, and tribological (friction and wear) properties of the resulting nano-grease or nano-lubricant materials [8–12]. Superior electrothermal conductivity of graphene is one of the most prominent properties in the discussion of its applications. This property can be very effective in graphene applications as a lubricant, and it shows the role of high thermal conductivity and cooling effect in the lubricant [13].

Furthermore, Graphene and GQDs Nano scrolls can efficiently reduce frictional loss in engines, turbines, and other machines. By the way, replacing traditional lubricants, GQDs could help the environment by replacing petroleum-based lubricants that must be disposed of biological disaster [14–18].

There are two main methods for synthesizing GQDs, i.e. top-down [19–21] and bottom-up. However, the bottom-up approaches are based on the construction of GQDs from small organic precursor molecules through catalytic or thermal treatment, resulting in the environment-friendly production of large scale GQDs with uniform size of distribution and morphology [22,23]. GQDs represent a new type of nanomaterial with a size generally less than 10 nm in diameter. Syntheses of GQDs are much easier compared with other quantum dots, and remarkably cheaper in handling and production cost [24,25]. Due to its exceptional properties, such as low toxicity [26,27], high chemical stability [28], excellent biocompatibility [29–31], high-fluorescence [32,33], considering these unique physical-chemical characteristics, GQDs have been applied broadly in fields of catalysis [34], printing ink [35], biological sensors [36–38], bioimaging [23] and drug delivery [39], GQDs are used as a novel material for biological, optical, energy, environmental and lubrication properties [40–42].

They were employed not only as solid lubricants to form protective membranes on certain substrates [43–45], but also as reinforced phases in various polymers [46], ceramics [47,48] and lubricating additives [49,50] in various base oils. Up to now, few studies have been conducted on their tribological applications [51,52] and it is still a rather challenging matter to achieve optimal combination for those base ingredients at nanoscale dimensions [53–56].

Layered lubricants are a large group of materials that exhibit excellent anti friction properties due to the layered structure and weakly bonded Van Der Walls inter layer interaction resulting capability of high load carrying performance [57,58]. Graphite, molybdenum disulfide and h-BN can exhibit lubricant properties. Graphite is superior to the rest due to its layered structure, which can slide on each other, resulting a protective layer that reduces friction and corrosion [59–62]. Graphite is an inert substance and does not dissolve or disperse in different solvents. In a research carried out by W Zhang and et al., they modified graphene sheets with oleic acid for oil additives, which ultimately resulted acceptable anti friction and wear properties [17].

Comparing the friction and wear behavior of graphite, graphene and graphene oxide showed the best anti-friction effect of graphene. The defect in graphene oxide is due to the presence of oxygenated groups at the surface which causes corrosion of steel. Nor does it provide two-dimensional coatings like graphene. The low anti-friction properties of graphite in comparison to graphene can be attributed to the sliding of graphite particles. Moreover, graphite is not capable of forming a thin protective layer such as graphene [44].

The GQDs is known as the next generation of carbon-based nanomaterials. In the synthesis with a bottom-up approach, GQDs have particle size totally uniform and controlled and have high surface area. Advantage of these nanomaterials is better than the graphene produced by exfoliation, which are not uniform in size and shape (Fig. 1 ). Also, these quantum dots can cover the defect and fraction present on the natural surface, and improve the properties of the lubricant [5,6].

Current research focuses on exploring synthetic routes in a more economical synthesis approach by utilizing green chemistry and diversifying possible starting precursors. Covering material was selected based on its ecofriendly nature which later will stand as a better tool in applications that require low toxicity tolerance. Therefore, we tried to synthesize functionalized GQD using the catalyst with high fluorescence at low temperature. The design in GQD synthesis is based on the following considerations: well surface passivation of GQDs plays a key role in quantum dots fabrication. In our research, we chose citric acid as the carbon source due to its well-known low carbonization temperature (<200 °C), and used octyl amine as the capping and functionalizing reagent because of functionalized and passivation surface states with carboxyl groups having a good photo-induced electron transfer property.

Citric acid, octyl amine, Iron (III) Chloride Hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O), Magnesium Oxide (MgO) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., all solvents were obtained from Merck Co. and used as received without further puriﬁcation. 1H NMR and 13C NMR spectra of the compound were obtained in CDCl3 using a FT-NMR Bruker (400 MHz) spectrometer. FTIR spectra were recorded using a Bruker rector 22 spectrophotometer. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the samples were obtained with a Bruker D8 Advance diffractometer using a Cu Kα source (λ = 0.154056 nm). Four ball tests were obtained from Alborz Tadbir Karan company. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured using a LEO TEM apparatus. The Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) of the samples was measured using Mettler Toledo instrument under N2 with a heating rate of 10 °C/min. Extreme pressure test was performed by portable Timken OK Tester, Linh Phan Co Ltd, Vietnam. DLS measurements was measured by Nanotrac Wave, Microtrac Co. Fluorescence spectra and intensity measurements were carried out using an FP-6200 spectrofluorometer (JASCO Corporation, Tokyo, Japan).

The Fe@MgO particles were synthesized as reported elsewhere [63–65]. Briefly, 5.0 g of FeCl3.6H2O was dissolved in ethanol (100 mL), and then MgO powder (10.0 g) was added under vigorous stirring. The reaction was exothermic and a brownish powder product was obtained after cooling, filtrating and drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of layered lipophilic graphene quantum dot (LGQD)
ce:section.ce:para: LGQD were synthesized by one-step thermal modified pyrolysis of citric acid in presence of octylamine over the catalyst. In a typical synthesis, Fe@MgO powders (0.3 g) were mixed with citric acid (3 g) and octylamine (3.8 ml) was placed in a one-neck round-bottom ﬂask. The reaction mixture was stirred at 165 °C for 2 h. The very viscous liquid was poured into cold methanol and the solid product was ﬁltered. In order to remove the metal and metal oxide impurities, the obtained powder was stirred in 1 M HCl solution at 80 °C for about 8 h, then the product washed with methanol and dried under vacuum.

The resulting material has a layer structure shown in Scheme 1 . FT-IR of product in Fig. 2 shows that broad peak in 2500–3500 cm−1, related to citric acid, are disappeared and the OH and NH groups around 3500 cm−1 and 3300 cm−1 appeared, respectively. The aliphatic CH of the lateral correction group appears at around 2900 cm−1. The peak at 1706 cm−1 is attributed to the C=O stretching vibration. FT-IR spectrum of LGQD represents that peaks related to main functional groups, OH and COOH, in citric acid were disappeared due to polymerization or carbonization during the reaction.

As seen in Fig. 3 , 1HNMR spectrum data of the synthesized graphene in chloroform matches well with the modified graphene, and the sharp peak in the near 1.25 ppm and peak around 7.5 ppm correspond to H atom connected to amine groups and the connection of octyl amine carbon groups on GQDs. 13CNMR spectroscopy can distinguish sp3 and sp2 hybridized carbons and indicate carboxyl/amide groups signals in the range of 170–185 ppm. 13CNMR in Fig. 4 , simply reveals that the alkyl groups are successfully connected to the surface of graphene.

One of the properties of GQDs is their fluorescence properties. Both the intensity and position of the fluorescence emission totally depend on the particle size [32,33,66]. The intensity decreases as the particle grows larger. Therefore, the size of the particle may be estimated by measuring the emission intensity. As shown in Fig. 5 , stimulating this GQD with a wavelength of 300 nm emits fluorescence light of at 490 nm. The fluorescence intensity indicates that the particle size is in quantum size.
Although we were expecting particles below 10 nm, the TEM, DLS, and XRD revealed that the present structure is of a unique characteristic.

Fig. 6 showed both XRD and expanded XRD spectra. XRD spectrum of this composition is completely different from those of graphene and GQD. A strong sharp peak is observed at 2θ = 2.7 and there appeared two other peaks exactly at positions two and three times the location of the first sharp peak which this order of peaks in such materials demonstrate layered structure [67]. This XRD pattern confirms that the synthesized GQD possesses a layered structure. From XRD it is revealed that the space between the two layers is around 3.1 nm [68,69].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transmission electron microscopy and DLS analysis of LGQD
ce:section.ce:para: As TEM microscopy reveals in Fig. 7 A, layered GQD is much larger than 10 nm in size (about 35 nm). DLS analysis on LGQDs (Fig. 7B) confirms that the size of the sample is 31.88 nm.

Thermogravimetric analysis graphs (Fig. 8 ) show weight profiles of LGQD temperature (heating rate, 10 °C/min) under N2 flow. The significant weight loss of 30.57% at 200 °C, is presumably due to the loss of those oxygen-containing groups before the complete oxidative decomposition of the LGQD over 280–700 °C.

Comparing the results of four ball test for the synthesized LGQD with commercial ZDDP shows that synthesized LGQD can pass the results four times better than ZDDP, as presented in Fig. 9 . These all admit that the synthesized compositions of considerable potential are a proper candidate for an extreme pressure additive. Different behavior of synthesized layered GQD against vertical and horizontal pressure are shown in Scheme 2 .

Timken OK Load is a standardized measurement that indicates the possible performance of extreme pressure (EP) additives in a lubricating grease or oil [70]. This test was performed for both standard and LGQD samples. The results indicated that LGQD played well against friction and had much better EP properties in comparison with standard.

Layered structures are able to absorb the oil into themselves and create a gel form. When the oil is added to the synthesized LGQD, the first operation is swelling without dissolving. After reaching the final swelling, the LGQD can be dissolved by increasing the oil (Fig. 10 ). To determine the swelling, dried LGQD powder (0.5 g) was swelled in oil and after complete swelling final volume of the swell was 74.79 cm3.

As in the introduction, GQDs have special properties that increase their increasing use in industry and medicine. In the current work, the aim is to provide a fast and easy synthesis method using a catalyst for the synthesis of layered GQDs with excellent lubricity properties. The results of FT-IR and CNMR and HNMR analyzes proved successful synthesis of LGQD linking with octylamine molecules. Based on the results of the fluorescence analysis of synthesized compound, the emergence of a strong peak at 490 nm at a wavelength of 300 nm, represents the quantum dot size for particles. Since quantum confinement depends on particle size [71–73], GQDs show different photoluminescence spectra as a function of their size [74,75]. The excitation must be strong enough to hide the absorption band associated with π-π∗ transitions [21]. Fluorescence of GQDs can because by carbon excitation, emissive traps, quantum confinement and edge effect, aromatic structure, oxygenated groups, zigzag free sites [33,76]. The strong fluorescence of GQDs is directly related to the size and structure of particles. The larger the size of the particles, and the intensity of the peak decreases and shifts to higher wavelengths [32,77–79]. According to the selective synthesis method for GQDs as well as selected precursors, the temperature control and the time of synthesis directly effects on particles size [80,81]. By controlling the above conditions and based on the results of fluorescence analysis, it is expected that the size of the synthesized particles is quantum dot. But in the TEM and DLS analyzes, the particle size is about 30 nm. This contradiction is justified by result of the XRD analysis. XRD patterns of synthetic product reflects the layered structure of the compounds. The presence of sharp and strong peaks in range of 2θ = 2–70 indicates good crystallinity and layered structure of the LQGD [82–85] which exhibits connection of several lipophilic GQDs through weak Van Der Walls forces between lipophilic GQD branches and construction of a layered structure. Therefore, the particle size in TEM and DLS exceeded the quantum dot limit. Due to the presence of lipophilic octylamine groups on the surface and between quantum dot particles of graphene, the resultant product is highly lipophilic and easily disperse in oil. Also, due to its layered structure, it has the ability to absorb oil in a massive amount and can be use as an additive in lubricants. The results of the Timken and four ball tests were used to study lubricity and EP properties of the synthesized product. It was found that the product has a superb lubricant and anti-friction properties.

As it stands out from the results, use of LGQD in friction is more appealing in harsh conditions like load step transition, high loads and high temperatures. Therefore, utility of lipophilic GQD additive in oil and lubricant is low-cost and green and friendly to environment. Its special properties make it applicable to use in high temperature and high-pressure conditions [86].

In this process, we linked GQD with octylamine to prepare octylamine layered GQD sheets (LGQD). The chemical and structural features of the LGQD sheets were examined by FTIR, XRD, 1H NMR, 13C NMR and TEM analyses. The long alkyl chains in the LGQD sheets made their stable dispersion in engine oil more efficiently, which is very important for their tribological performance. Due to layered and lipophilic structure of LGQD, we assume that the tribological test will show the ability of LGQD sheets to reduce friction and improve the anti-wear properties under the rolling contacts.

Excellent performance of the LGQD as lubricant additive is attributed to (a) the weak van der Waals interaction between the lamellas of the LGQD sheets, which provides the low resistance to shear under the rolling contact stress, and (b) the continuous supply of the LGQD sheets on the contact surfaces due to their stable dispersion in the lube oil [36]. Also, four-ball tests confirm the results correctness which was necessary in order to compare LGQD potential with the most common used commercial extreme pressure additive (EP additive) ZDDP (Zinc dialkyldithiophosphates). Best performance LGQD on EP test was compared with SN 150 base oil containing 1% ZDDP in which the results are shown in Fig. 9.
E.R was the main executive of research and prepared and analyzed the data, M.M was the supervisor of the group.
This research received no external funding

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the office of the research vice chancellor of Azarbaijan Shahid Madani University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.matchemphys.2019.04.073.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.
